learning_objectives: Describe the organization of the nervous system: central and peripheral divisions
Identify the major cell types: neurons and glia
Explain the basic principles of neural signaling
readings: Bear, Connors, & Paradiso, Neuroscience: Exploring the Brain, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Neuroscience is the study of the nervous system -- the biological machinery that generates every thought, feeling, and action. This course provides a foundational understanding accessible to students from psychology, biology, and other disciplines.
key_concepts: CNS (brain and spinal cord) vs. PNS (somatic and autonomic)
Neuron structure: dendrites, soma, axon, synaptic terminals
Glial cells: astrocytes, oligodendrocytes, microglia, Schwann cells
Electrical signaling: resting potential and action potential basics
Chemical signaling: neurotransmission at the synapse
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a neuron model using household materials: identify structural components and signal direction
setup_requirements: Neuron model materials (pipe cleaners, beads, clay)

review.value: Brain and spinal cord: the integrative and command center; protected by skull, vertebral column, meninges, and cerebrospinal fluid
Support cells that outnumber neurons: astrocytes regulate the blood-brain barrier and synaptic environment; oligodendrocytes/Schwann cells form myelin for rapid signal conduction

learning_objectives: Explain the ionic basis of the resting membrane potential
Describe the phases of the action potential and the ions involved
Explain saltatory conduction and the role of myelin

lecture_notes.introduction: Neural communication begins with electrical signals. Understanding the action potential -- the fundamental unit of neural signaling -- provides the foundation for all of neuroscience.
key_concepts: Resting potential (~-70mV): maintained by Na+/K+ ATPase pump and selective permeability
Depolarization: Na+ channels open, Na+ rushes in
Repolarization: K+ channels open, K+ rushes out
Refractory periods: absolute (no firing) and relative (harder to fire)
Saltatory conduction: action potential jumps between nodes of Ranvier in myelinated axons
lecture_notes.demonstration: Interactive simulation: manipulate ion concentrations and channel properties to observe effects on the action potential

review.value: Once threshold is reached, the action potential fires at full strength regardless of stimulus intensity; information is coded by firing rate, not amplitude
Myelin insulation forces the action potential to jump between nodes of Ranvier, increasing conduction speed 50-100x and reducing metabolic cost

learning_objectives: Describe the steps of chemical synaptic transmission
Identify major neurotransmitter systems and their functions
Explain excitatory and inhibitory postsynaptic potentials

lecture_notes.introduction: Synapses are where neurons communicate. The chemical synapse converts electrical signals to chemical signals and back, providing the flexibility needed for learning and adaptation.
key_concepts: Steps: AP reaches terminal, Ca2+ influx, vesicle fusion, neurotransmitter release, receptor binding, reuptake/degradation
Excitatory PSPs (glutamate, AMPA/NMDA) vs. inhibitory PSPs (GABA, GABA-A)
Temporal and spatial summation of PSPs
Major systems: glutamate, GABA, dopamine, serotonin, norepinephrine, acetylcholine
Drug mechanisms: agonists enhance, antagonists block, reuptake inhibitors prolong
lecture_notes.demonstration: Synapse in action: students role-play components of synaptic transmission (vesicle, neurotransmitter, receptor, transporter)

review.value: EPSP: depolarization that makes firing more likely (glutamate). IPSP: hyperpolarization that makes firing less likely (GABA). The neuron integrates thousands of these to decide whether to fire.
Mesolimbic (reward, motivation), mesocortical (executive function), nigrostriatal (movement), tuberoinfundibular (prolactin regulation)

learning_objectives: Identify major brain regions and their primary functions
Describe the organization of the cerebral cortex into lobes and functional areas
Explain methods for studying brain function: lesions, imaging, stimulation

lecture_notes.introduction: The brain's structure reflects its function. Knowing brain anatomy allows us to predict deficits from damage and interpret neuroimaging findings.
key_concepts: Brainstem: medulla (vital functions), pons (sleep, arousal), midbrain (auditory/visual reflexes)
Cerebellum: coordination, timing, motor learning
Diencephalon: thalamus (sensory relay), hypothalamus (homeostasis, hormones)
Limbic system: hippocampus (memory), amygdala (emotion), cingulate cortex
Cortical lobes: frontal (executive), parietal (somatosensory), temporal (auditory, memory), occipital (vision)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Virtual brain dissection: identify 15 structures and predict the consequences of damage to each

review.value: Contains primary motor cortex, Broca's area (speech production), and prefrontal cortex (planning, decision-making, personality, working memory)
Essential for forming new explicit memories; damage causes anterograde amnesia (inability to form new memories) while procedural memory remains intact

learning_objectives: Describe the pathways for vision and somatosensation from receptor to cortex
Explain the organization of the motor system: cortex, basal ganglia, cerebellum
Discuss sensory processing principles: topographic maps, receptive fields, hierarchical processing

lecture_notes.introduction: Sensory and motor systems reveal fundamental principles of neural organization: topographic mapping, hierarchical processing, and parallel pathways.
key_concepts: Visual pathway: retina -> LGN -> V1 -> dorsal (where/how) and ventral (what) streams
Somatosensory pathway: receptors -> dorsal column nuclei -> thalamus (VPL) -> S1
Somatotopic maps: homunculus representing body surface in cortex
Motor pathway: M1 -> corticospinal tract -> spinal motor neurons
Basal ganglia: movement initiation (Parkinson's = too little, Huntington's = too much)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Two-point discrimination test: map somatosensory acuity across body regions and compare to the homunculus

review.value: The area of the sensory surface (e.g., retina, skin) that, when stimulated, changes a neuron's firing rate; receptive fields increase in size and complexity at higher processing levels

Dorsal ('where/how'): processes spatial location and guides action. Ventral ('what'): processes object identity and form. Double dissociation evidence from brain-damaged patients confirms independence

class_schedule.topic: Neuroscience of Learning, Memory, and Neurological Disorders
learning_objectives: Explain synaptic plasticity as the cellular basis of learning
Describe the neuroscience of common neurological and psychiatric disorders
Discuss emerging neuroscience technologies and their ethical implications

lecture_notes.introduction: The brain's ability to change in response to experience -- neural plasticity -- underlies all learning and recovery from injury. Understanding plasticity also illuminates what goes wrong in neurological disorders.
key_concepts: Long-term potentiation (LTP) and long-term depression (LTD)
Hebbian learning: cells that fire together wire together
Neuroplasticity across the lifespan: critical periods, adult neurogenesis in hippocampus
Neurodegenerative disorders: Alzheimer's (amyloid/tau), Parkinson's (dopamine loss in substantia nigra)
Emerging technologies: optogenetics, CRISPR, brain-computer interfaces; ethical considerations
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study analysis: trace the neural basis of symptoms in an Alzheimer's patient from molecular to behavioral level

review.value: Persistent strengthening of synapses following high-frequency stimulation; requires NMDA receptor activation and calcium-dependent signaling cascades
Progressive neurodegeneration: amyloid-beta plaques and tau neurofibrillary tangles; begins in entorhinal cortex/hippocampus (memory), spreads to cortex (cognition, personality)

questions.question: Describe the sequence of events during an action potential, from resting potential through return to rest. Include the specific ions and channels involved at each phase.
questions.answer: At rest (-70mV): K+ leak channels keep the membrane polarized; Na+/K+ ATPase maintains the gradient. (1) Stimulus depolarizes membrane to threshold (~-55mV). (2) Voltage-gated Na+ channels open rapidly: Na+ rushes in, depolarizing to ~+30mV (rising phase). (3) Na+ channels inactivate (ball-and-chain mechanism). Voltage-gated K+ channels open (delayed): K+ rushes out, repolarizing the membrane (falling phase). (4) K+ channels close slowly, causing brief hyperpolarization below -70mV (undershoot). (5) Na+/K+ ATPase restores ion gradients; membrane returns to resting potential.

rubric.key: Correctly describes repolarization (K+ efflux) and Na+ inactivation
Mentions hyperpolarization and return to rest

questions.question: Explain why blocking the reuptake of serotonin (as SSRIs do) increases serotonergic signaling. Describe the specific synaptic mechanism.
questions.answer: Normally, after serotonin is released into the synaptic cleft and binds to postsynaptic receptors, it is removed from the cleft primarily by serotonin transporters (SERT) on the presynaptic neuron -- this is reuptake. SSRIs (selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors) block the SERT transporter, preventing serotonin from being recycled back into the presynaptic terminal. As a result, serotonin remains in the cleft longer and continues to bind and activate postsynaptic receptors. This prolongs and intensifies serotonergic signaling. Over weeks, this leads to receptor downregulation and other adaptive changes that are thought to mediate the antidepressant effect.

rubric.key: Explains SSRI blockade of serotonin transporter
Notes prolonged receptor activation and/or downstream adaptations

questions.question: A patient suffers a stroke in the left hemisphere affecting Broca's area. Describe the expected language deficit, distinguish it from Wernicke's aphasia, and explain why the lesion location produces this specific pattern.
questions.answer: Broca's aphasia (expressive/non-fluent aphasia): the patient can understand language (comprehension is relatively intact) but has great difficulty producing speech. Output is effortful, telegraphic, and agrammatic (e.g., 'Want... coffee... now'). Wernicke's aphasia (receptive/fluent aphasia) results from damage to Wernicke's area in the left temporal lobe: the patient produces fluent but meaningless speech and has severely impaired comprehension. The lesion location explains the pattern because Broca's area (left inferior frontal gyrus) is adjacent to the motor cortex controlling the mouth and larynx, and is responsible for speech motor programming and grammatical processing. Wernicke's area is in the auditory association cortex, responsible for speech comprehension and semantic processing.

rubric.key: Explains anatomical basis for the pattern

questions.question: Draw or describe the visual pathway from the retina to the primary visual cortex. Include at least four structures in order.
questions.answer: The visual pathway: (1) Light is transduced by photoreceptors (rods and cones) in the retina. (2) Retinal ganglion cell axons form the optic nerve. (3) The two optic nerves partially cross at the optic chiasm (nasal fibers cross, temporal fibers do not). (4) Post-chiasm, the optic tract projects to the lateral geniculate nucleus (LGN) of the thalamus. (5) From the LGN, axons travel via the optic radiations to the primary visual cortex (V1) in the occipital lobe. Key principle: each hemisphere receives input from the contralateral visual field. The LGN preserves retinotopic organization, which is maintained in V1.

rubric.key: Lists structures in correct order (retina, optic nerve, chiasm, LGN, V1)
Notes retinotopic organization or contralateral processing

major_assignments.title: Neuroscience of a Brain Disorder
major_assignments.description: Choose a neurological or psychiatric disorder and write a comprehensive review of its neuroscience. Cover the neural circuits, neurotransmitter systems, structural/functional brain changes, genetic and environmental risk factors, and current treatments with their mechanisms of action.
objectives: Demonstrate understanding of neural circuit dysfunction in a specific disorder
Integrate molecular, cellular, and systems-level neuroscience
Evaluate current treatments in terms of their neural mechanisms
Communicate complex neuroscience clearly for an educated non-specialist audience
deliverables: Brain diagram highlighting affected regions and circuits
Summary table of treatments and their mechanisms

criteria: Accurate description of neural circuits involved
Includes structural and functional brain changes
Explains mechanism of action at the neural level
Comprehensive and accurate neuroscience content across multiple levels of analysis
Thorough treatment review with mechanism of action explanations

lab_exercises.description: Neuroanatomy, simulation, and brain imaging exercises

midterm_examination.description: Exam covering neural signaling and brain anatomy

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all neuroscience topics

course_policies.attendance: Lab attendance is mandatory. Lecture attendance is strongly recommended.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. Lab reports must be submitted by the next lab session.
course_policies.collaboration: Lab exercises may be completed in pairs. All written assignments must be independently authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original with proper citations. Fabrication of lab data will result in course failure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to neuroanatomy software, brain simulation tools, and university library databases.
